policy_statement: Incorporate creeks and waterways as focal points for recreation, education, and off-street transportation within and near neighborhoods.
mechanism_description: It incorporates creeks and waterways as off-street transportation corridors to support more walking and biking instead of driving, reducing transportation-related emissions.
primary_causal_pathway: Transport infrastructure change → mode shift (↓ VMT, ↑ walking/biking) → ↓ fossil fuel combustion → ↓ GHG emissions → ↓ atmospheric forcing
causal_mechanism_detail: (1) Change: The city builds/designates connected, safe, off-street active transportation corridors (e.g., along creeks/waterways) that make walking and biking viable for more trips. (2) Mediating process: Improved convenience/safety/connectivity induces a mode shift away from private vehicles and/or shortens car trips, reducing vehicle miles traveled and associated gasoline/diesel combustion. (3) Outcome: Lower CO₂ (and some co-pollutant) emissions from the transportation sector.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is reduced on-road motor vehicle travel and fuel use, leading to lower transportation GHG emissions.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: active-transport corridor development drives mode shift and reduced VMT, which directly lowers fossil fuel combustion emissions.  
2) Why Mitigation over alternatives: while such corridors can improve health, recreation, and sometimes stormwater/heat conditions, the primary climate-relevant effect across policies using this mechanism is emissions reduction from transportation.  
3) Edge cases applied: if corridors are framed as riparian restoration/green infrastructure primarily for cooling/flood management, that could lean toward Nature-Based Solutions/Adaptation, but this mechanism is explicitly about off-street transport corridors enabling walking/biking instead of driving.  
4) Applicability to ALL instances: any policy that develops active-transport corridors to replace car trips shares the same main physical outcome—reduced motorized travel and emissions—so Mitigation is consistently primary.  
5) Secondary subtype deferred: whether the policy also delivers adaptation (e.g., flood-safe routes) or nature-based co-benefits (riparian enhancement) requires policy-specific evidence and is not assigned at the mechanism level.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: If a given corridor project is primarily an ecosystem restoration or flood-control project with only incidental transport function, the dominant pathway could shift to Nature-Based Solutions or Adaptation; confirm stated intent and quantified outcomes (mode shift/VMT) at the policy level.
key_indicators: “Strategy”; “Incorporate creeks and waterways”; “focal points”; “off-street transportation”; “within and abutting neighborhoods”
co_benefits: Recreation and public health; environmental education; local ecosystem/stream corridor stewardship; potential stormwater/floodplain co-management benefits (not explicitly stated).
instance_edge_case_notes: Could be classified as infrastructure-investment if the broader plan pairs this strategy with committed trail/greenway capital projects; however, the text itself is framed as a strategy (directional land use/transport integration) without specifying funding, construction, or enforceable requirements.
